sector: Technology / Power Semiconductors & Modules (point-of-load power delivery)
bull_thesis: Vicor owns the foundational patents for Vertical Power Delivery (VPD) — a power architecture that places its modules directly beneath next-generation AI graphics processors (GPUs) to deliver power at sub-1.6 volts across the final 1.5 millimeters of the power chain, the point where losses are highest. No competing approach delivers more than 1,500 watts efficiently at that depth. A February 2025 ruling by the U.S. International Trade Commission (ITC) — an agency that can bar imports of infringing products — validated two Vicor patents (9,516,761 and 9,166,481) and issued a Limited Exclusion Order barring competing, unlicensed computing systems from U.S. import. That ruling converted Vicor from a niche module maker into a product business plus a legal toll booth: leading original equipment manufacturers and hyperscale cloud companies were forced to take licenses, generating roughly $300 million in cumulative licensing revenue through 2026, and royalty income in the fiscal year ended 2025 reached $57.4 million, up 23 percent year over year. The commercial signal is now clear: first-quarter 2026 revenue was $113.0 million, up 20.2 percent year over year; gross margin reached 55.2 percent, up 800 basis points (each basis point is one-hundredth of a percentage point) from a year earlier; backlog stood at $300.6 million, up 70 percent from the prior quarter and 75 percent from a year ago; and the book-to-bill ratio (new orders divided by revenue shipped) exceeded 2.0, meaning Vicor is booking twice as much new business as it ships. Full-year 2026 guidance is approximately $570 million. The balance sheet is a fortress: $402.8 million in cash, effectively no debt, and roughly $119 million in free cash flow (operating cash minus capital expenditures) in fiscal 2025 — so the ongoing capacity ramp (Fab One, scalable to $1.5 billion in annual capacity, plus a second facility through a building acquisition) is entirely self-funded. Second-generation VPD delivers three amps per square millimeter and up to 40 times current multiplication in a 1.5-millimeter package, designed to sit directly beneath Nvidia Vera Rubin-class GPUs — the fastest-growing and most physically constrained layer of the AI infrastructure build-out. If the backlog converts and VPD becomes the standard point-of-load topology for Rubin and Kyber accelerators (expected in 2027), Vicor earns a durable, high-margin, architecture-locked franchise that the current valuation is already reaching for.
bear_thesis: The stock has quintupled in twelve months — up 494 percent over the trailing twelve months (TTM) and 145 to 179 percent year to date — to roughly 108 times forward earnings (price-to-earnings, or P/E) and more than 20 times forward revenue. The AI power-delivery optionality is already in the price, and then some. The 2025 earnings quality rests heavily on litigation windfalls: a one-time $45 million settlement and a 23 percent step-up in royalty income powered the headline 26 percent revenue growth, while the underlying product business grew far more slowly. First-quarter 2026 earnings per share of $0.44 on a generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) basis were reported alongside a $27.3 million tax benefit — stripping that out, pre-tax operating income was only approximately $16.9 million. The claim that licensing revenue runs at 'nearly 100 percent margin' inadvertently reveals that the product gross margin is materially below the blended 55 percent figure. Litigation is a two-way street: Vicor itself paid SynQor $28.6 million in March 2026 after a Federal Circuit ruling went against it. The roughly $300 million in cumulative licensing is described as running 'through 2026,' with no disclosed run-rate thereafter; new deals are explicitly gated on a 2027 ITC proceeding, so the royalty annuity depends on future legal outcomes, not just commercial demand. Critically, the ITC ruling that drove the re-rating already happened in February 2025 — that catalyst has already fired and the forced-licensing wave is behind the stock. Chief executive Patrizio Vinciarelli's public campaign against a competing '800-volt-to-6-volt' power architecture signals that the point-of-load topology debate is live and unresolved industry-wide; if hyperscalers standardize on a non-Vicor approach, VPD's addressable product market shrinks even with the patents intact. Founder dual-class share control limits outside governance oversight. A name priced for two to three years of flawless execution is deeply asymmetric to the downside on any conversion stumble.
sop_bull: Approximately $330 to $360 per share, representing an intrinsic value estimate for the whole company on a high-quality intellectual property plus product-ramp base. The bull framework: fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $570 million growing toward a $700 million-plus run-rate as the $300.6 million backlog ships. Assume a mature blended operating margin of 28 to 30 percent on $700 million — approximately $200 million in operating income and roughly $165 million in net income at a normalized tax rate. On approximately 46 million diluted shares, that implies a run-rate earnings per share of approximately $3.60. A premium multiple of 90 to 95 times forward earnings — in line with where high-scarcity AI-exposed intellectual-property compounders like Monolithic Power Systems have traded at peak AI enthusiasm — produces approximately $325 to $340, plus roughly $9 per share in net cash ($403 million divided by 46 million shares). Adding an intellectual-property annuity premium (for the perpetual high-margin royalty stream that Vicor's 2027 ITC leverage could extend) worth approximately $10 to $15 per share produces a bull-case range of $345 to $360.
sop_bear: Approximately $110 to $140 per share. Strip the one-time windfalls and value only the durable productive franchise. The bear framework: normalize out the $45 million one-time settlement and treat royalties as a decaying annuity with no guaranteed post-2026 renewal. Normalized productive earnings power works out to roughly $0.90 to $1.20 per share (based on fiscal 2025 figures excluding the tax benefit and the settlement run-rate). On a still-rich but defensible 40 to 45 times multiple — consistent with the power-semiconductor peer group of Power Integrations and Monolithic Power Systems, which have de-rated from peak — that yields approximately $45 to $55 from operations, plus roughly $9 per share in net cash, plus a conservative $50 to $70 per share for the genuinely validated intellectual property and VPD optionality and backlog conversion. Total: approximately $110 to $140. This is what remains if the architecture war turns against VPD or the earnings multiple compresses to cohort levels.
sop_breakdown: The base case blends three components. First, the productive franchise: fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $570 million growing toward a $650 million run-rate at roughly 26 percent operating margin produces approximately $150 million in operating income and approximately $120 million in net income on roughly 46 million shares, or approximately $2.60 in normalized earnings per share; at a 60 to 65 times multiple that reflects AI intellectual-property premium without assuming peak enthusiasm, that yields approximately $160 to $170 per share. Second, net cash of approximately $9 per share (the $403 million cash balance on a debt-free balance sheet). Third, an intellectual-property and royalty annuity overlay — haircut for post-2026 uncertainty and the offsetting SynQor payment — worth approximately $35 to $50 per share. Sum: approximately $205 to $230, with a point estimate of roughly $215. The wide gap between the bull case ($345 to $360) and the bear case ($110 to $140) is itself the conclusion: the value is real, but at $328 the market is paying for the bull tail scenario, not the base case.
risk_reward: Unfavorable. At approximately $328 versus a base-case sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business component separately) of roughly $215, the downside to fair value is approximately negative 34 percent; the downside to the bear break is approximately negative 58 to 66 percent. Upside to the bull case is only about positive 5 to 10 percent. The risk-to-reward ratio is roughly 6 to 1 against on a base-case lens. The asymmetry is to the downside because the re-rating already happened.
peer_comps_note: The truest scale-out peers within the AI power-delivery semiconductor group are Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) — the closest AI-power comparable, trading at roughly 52 times next-twelve-months enterprise value to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EV/EBITDA) and about 59 times forward price-to-earnings as of June 2026, representing the richest multiple in the cohort, and a far larger ($30 billion-plus) and more diversified franchise with direct GPU voltage regulator module market share; Power Integrations (POWI) — high-voltage and gallium-nitride (GaN) focused, lower growth, value-cohort multiple; Navitas Semiconductor (NVTS) — GaN and silicon carbide (SiC) pure-play, speculative, benchmarked against Monolithic Power, Power Integrations, and Infineon; Infineon (IFX) and onsemi (ON) — broad-line SiC and power semiconductors trading at roughly 15 to 25 times EV/EBITDA; Texas Instruments (TXN) at roughly 24 times and Analog Devices (ADI) at roughly 20 times EV/EBITDA anchor the analog semiconductor floor; Nvidia at roughly 17 times and Broadcom at roughly 19 times show that even the largest AI-infrastructure names trade far cheaper than the power-semiconductor cohort. Ranked within this group, Vicor carries the highest valuation in the entire complex — approximately 108 times forward price-to-earnings and more than 20 times forward revenue — on the smallest revenue base ($570 million in fiscal 2026 guidance versus Monolithic Power's multi-billion-dollar scale). Vicor ranks first on AI-capital-expenditure purity and intellectual-property criticality, and its gross margin trajectory (55 percent) is attractive, but it ranks last on valuation and scale. A portion of its returns are litigation-sourced rather than purely from commercial execution. The peer group itself has re-rated to elevated multiples on AI enthusiasm; Vicor sits at the extreme end of that re-rating.
role_in_scaleout: Vicor owns the intellectual property for the 'last 1.5 millimeters' of the AI rack power chain. Its Vertical Power Delivery architecture places modules directly beneath 1,500-watt-plus AI GPUs to deliver sub-1.6-volt core power at roughly 50 percent lower loss than conventional horizontal delivery, making it the most physically constrained and difficult-to-substitute point in the power delivery path. Demand durability is high as long as VPD remains the standard point-of-load topology for Rubin-class accelerators, but that outcome is contingent on winning the ongoing architecture debate — it is not guaranteed by the patents alone.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market: Wall Street is not looking the wrong way in the contrarian sense this strategy seeks. Analysts and investors are looking directly at this name and have bid it to roughly 108 times forward earnings, with consensus price targets near $282 (Needham at $350) and a year-to-date gain of 145 to 179 percent. The expectations edge is inverted: instead of the market under-pricing a hidden catalyst, the market has fully priced — and arguably over-priced — the AI power-monopoly thesis. The one nuance the consensus may be soft-pedaling is earnings quality: the $27.3 million tax benefit inside first-quarter 2026 earnings per share and the $45 million one-time fiscal 2025 settlement mean the productive run-rate is lower than the reported figures imply, and the 'through 2026' framing of the roughly $300 million in cumulative licensing leaves the post-2026 royalty annuity undefined. That is a downside-revealing nuance, not a hidden bull catalyst. There is no mispriced asymmetric upside remaining.
valley_of_death: The risky window spans the next three to nine months and has four main hazards. First, earnings-quality reset: the next reported quarters will lap a $27.3 million tax benefit and a non-recurring $45 million settlement; reported growth and earnings per share could decelerate sharply on a clean comparison even as the underlying business expands — a classic multiple-compression trigger at 108 times earnings. Second, capital expenditure inflection: the planned scale-up of Fab One to $1.5 billion in capacity and the addition of a second facility imply a meaningful increase in spending that will pressure the currently strong free cash flow as it materializes. Third, architecture-war headline risk: any signal from a hyperscaler favoring a competing '800-volt-to-6-volt' or non-VPD point-of-load topology — the architecture Vinciarelli publicly dismissed — would hit the product addressable-market narrative sharply. Fourth, AI capital-expenditure is the single shared macro driver for the entire power-semiconductor basket; it accounts for roughly 40 percent of estimated S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026 and is the dominant two-sided risk. Any reduction in hyperscaler capital-expenditure guidance would broadly pressure the group, and a name at 108 times earnings has the most valuation to lose.
positioning_washout: Forced-selling pressure: this is a momentum- and growth-fund-owned name (up 494 percent over the trailing twelve months), not a yield or value base, so there is no dividend-cut or passive-yield forced-selling dynamic. The washout risk runs in the opposite direction: a crowded fast-money and AI-thematic shareholder base that would sell mechanically on the first earnings-quality miss or AI-capital-expenditure scare, with little valuation support beneath the current price ($403 million in net cash is only roughly $9 per share against a $328 stock — approximately 3 percent of market capitalization). The absence of a value floor means a de-rating could be swift and deep. There is no meaningful forced-buyer offset.
forcing_function: The catalyst that forces the re-rating: the most significant hard catalysts are mostly behind the stock. The February 2025 ITC Final Determination and Limited Exclusion Order have already fired and drove the bulk of the licensing settlements and the price re-rating. Forward events that could move the stock: (1) the 2027 ITC litigation resolution that gates new licensing deals — real but distant, and a two-sided binary; (2) Vera Rubin production ramp milestones — air-cooled samples mid-2026, liquid-cooled Ultra late-2026, full 800-volt DC with Kyber in 2027 — where design-win confirmations could extend the narrative but are largely anticipated; (3) the clean-comparison quarters in the second half of 2026 when the tax-benefit and settlement tailwinds roll off — the most likely near-term de-rating trigger. None of these represents an undiscovered, asymmetric upside catalyst.
moderator_conclusion: Applying the June 2026 market regime — characterized by complacent melt-up conditions, AI capital-expenditure as the load-bearing driver of roughly 40 percent of estimated S&P 500 earnings growth, and a mandate to reward only catalysts that are both contractual and independent of the AI-capex single factor: Vicor is a genuinely excellent franchise. It holds court-validated foundational intellectual property at the most physically inescapable point in the AI power chain, carries a wide and widening competitive moat (55 percent gross margin, near-100-percent-margin royalties, $403 million in net cash, roughly $119 million in free cash flow, backlog up 70 percent from the prior quarter with a book-to-bill ratio above 2.0). The analyst credibility rating of 4 out of 5 and a strengthening trajectory are well-supported. But the role of a disciplined portfolio overseer here is to assess timing and margin of safety, and on both measures this is a pass. First, the load-bearing ITC catalyst already fired in February 2025 — under the current regime mandate, already-fired catalysts that have driven a full re-rating are penalized, not rewarded; the stock has quintupled on it and the re-rating is spent. Second, at roughly 108 times forward earnings and more than 20 times revenue, the stock trades above even a generous base-case sum-of-the-parts estimate of roughly $215 versus the current $328 — risk-to-reward is approximately 6 to 1 against, with no value floor (net cash is only about 3 percent of market capitalization). Third, earnings quality is flattered by litigation income and tax timing, and the cleanest second-half 2026 comparisons are a de-rating forcing function rather than an upside one. Fourth, and critically for maintaining book independence: Vicor's entire thesis depends on the same single shared macro factor — AI capital-expenditure durability — as the rest of the power-semiconductor basket and much of the broader index; adding this name adds concentration, not diversification. The value case absent the AI overlay is weak — you do not pay 108 times earnings for a $570 million-revenue power-module company whose productive margins fall well below the royalty-blended 55 percent headline figure. Verdict: pass for now, conviction 2. This is a buy-on-the-dip-someday name, not an entry at this price. The architecture is real, but the price has front-run two to three years of flawless execution through a still-contested point-of-load architecture debate. Watchlist only; revisit after a meaningful AI-capital-expenditure flush that resets the multiple toward the sum-of-the-parts estimate.
interrogator_dossier:
Forensic Dossier: Vicor Corporation (NASDAQ: VICR)

Scale-out Basket D (power semiconductors). Power modules and 800-volt-relevant point-of-load power delivery. Prepared in the Interrogator role. Data live-verified June 2026; no earnings-call transcript bundle was provided, so financials and management commentary were sourced from the first-quarter 2026 earnings call, the fiscal 2025 annual report on Form 10-K and related Form 8-K filings, and ITC docket materials.

Narrative Arc

Vicor's story has undergone one of the cleanest transformations in the power-semiconductor complex over the past two years. The company that for a decade was a sleepy, capacity-constrained, gross-margin-volatile maker of high-density power modules — perennially 'about to inflect' on a 48-volt data-center transition that kept slipping — has been recast as the owner of the foundational intellectual property for Vertical Power Delivery and the Factorized Power Architecture: the physical method by which the final 1.5 millimeters of power reach a 1,500-watt-plus AI GPU at sub-1.6-volt core voltage. Three phases drove this transformation. First, intellectual-property awakening (2024 through February 2025): the February 2025 ITC Final Determination validated U.S. Patents 9,516,761 and 9,166,481 and issued a Limited Exclusion Order, converting a product company into product plus legal toll booth. Second, royalty harvest (2025): fiscal 2025 royalty revenue reached $57.4 million (up 23.2 percent year over year) plus a one-time $45 million settlement, lifting total fiscal 2025 revenue up 26.1 percent to $452.7 million; cumulative licensing through 2026 reached roughly $300 million. Third, VPD product ramp (2026 onward): second-generation VPD at three amps per square millimeter, up to 40 times current multiplication, 1.5-millimeter package, positioned under Vera Rubin GPUs; first-quarter 2026 revenue $113.0 million (up 20.2 percent year over year); backlog $300.6 million (up 70 percent from the prior quarter, up 75 percent year over year); book-to-bill above 2.0; fiscal 2026 guidance approximately $570 million. Quietly dropped from the narrative: capacity and margin hand-wringing. Added: a confrontational posture — chief executive Vinciarelli dismissing the competing '800-volt-to-6-volt' architecture as 'ill-conceived' and 'structurally misguided.'

Claims Versus Financials (Forensic)

Demand capacity-constrained, book-to-bill above 2.0: verified — backlog $300.6 million up 70 percent from the prior quarter, fiscal 2026 guidance $570 million. Gross margin expanding structurally: partially verified — 55.2 percent versus 47.2 percent one year earlier (up 800 basis points), but declining sequentially from 55.4 percent; second-quarter 2025 spiked to 65.3 percent on favorable royalty mix, which is not repeatable at that level. Licensing near 100 percent margin: verified and central to the thesis — profit quality is litigation-derived. Net income inflecting: overstated — first-quarter 2026 earnings per share of $0.44 include a $27.3 million tax benefit; pre-tax operating income was only approximately $16.9 million. Fortress balance sheet: verified — $402.8 million cash, debt-free, fiscal 2025 operating cash flow $139.5 million, capital expenditures $20.3 million, free cash flow roughly $119 million. Capacity scalable to $1.5 billion: plausible but unproven. The key divergence: the AI-monopoly narrative is richer than today's productive financials. Fiscal 2025's 26 percent revenue growth was carried by a $45 million one-time settlement and a 23 percent royalty step-up; the product business grew far more slowly.

Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rising, and assertively so. Vinciarelli's register in the first-quarter 2026 earnings call is the most offensive in company history — publicly disparaging competing architectures as having 'inadequate current density' and 'stacked packages that are not mechanically and thermally adept,' reframing product scarcity as pricing power. Risk: founder-controlled overreach, and the polemic against the 800-volt-to-6-volt approach signals that the architecture debate is live and not settled in Vicor's favor across the industry.

Guidance Credibility

Medium. First-quarter 2026 earnings per share beat the prior guidance range ($0.44 to $0.52 versus the guided $0.37 to $0.41); fiscal 2026 guidance of approximately $570 million is concrete. But the pre-2025 track record was one of repeated 48-volt-inflection deferrals; the credibility upgrade rests on the exogenous ITC outcome rather than sustained commercial execution. Royalty and settlement income is lumpy and partly non-recurring (the $45 million settlement will not repeat; Vicor itself paid SynQor $28.6 million in March 2026). The product ramp is unproven at the new scale. Overall assessment: medium credibility, trending upward only if the backlog converts.

Analyst Pressure Points

Litigation dependence — the royalty versus product revenue split is not broken out clearly by management; customer-concentration and design-win confirmation — Vera Rubin VPD is inferred from the import leverage the ITC order created rather than disclosed customer contracts; capacity and capital-expenditure trajectory — a $1.5 billion facility implies a future capital-expenditure step-up not yet visible in cash flows; royalty durability post-2026 — the roughly $300 million in cumulative licensing is described as running 'through 2026' with no defined run-rate thereafter, and new deals await a 2027 ITC resolution.

Red Flags and Green Flags

Green flags: net-cash, debt-free balance sheet ($403 million); court-validated foundational intellectual property (ITC Limited Exclusion Order); backlog up 70 percent from the prior quarter with book-to-bill above 2.0; gross margin up 800 basis points year over year. Red flags: earnings quality (first-quarter 2026 earnings per share flattered by a $27.3 million tax benefit; fiscal 2025 growth flattered by a $45 million settlement); valuation (roughly 108 times forward earnings, more than 20 times revenue, up 494 percent over the trailing twelve months); litigation cuts both ways (Vicor paid SynQor $28.6 million); the architecture debate between VPD and 800-volt-to-6-volt remains unresolved; founder dual-class governance.

Hidden Signals

The shift from describing capacity constraints as an apology into framing them as a selectivity and pricing-power lever is the key tonal tell. Guidance explicitly excludes new licensing deals pending the 2027 ITC resolution — simultaneously a potential sandbag and a flag that royalty durability hinges on future legal outcomes. The pivot back toward product (VPD technical specifications) after a year leaning on royalty income suggests management knows the litigation windfall is not a permanent revenue base and is racing to convert intellectual-property leverage into shipped silicon before the architecture war is decided.

Capital-Allocation Assessment for the Portfolio Director

Trajectory: strengthening — off a base whose quality is mixed (litigation-heavy 2025, tax-assisted first-quarter 2026) and whose price has already discounted the strengthening. The single most important variable to weigh: how much AI-power-delivery optionality is already embedded in roughly 108 times forward earnings and more than 20 times revenue for a stock that quintupled in twelve months. Vicor owns real, court-validated foundational intellectual property at the most physically constrained point in the AI power chain — a genuinely wide-feeling competitive moat. But the productive ramp that would justify that multiple is still ahead and partly dependent on an unresolved point-of-load architecture debate and future litigation outcomes. A great franchise at a price that has front-run two to three years of perfect execution. Moat sources: patent-based intangibles plus the ITC regulatory exclusion order plus architecture lock-in switching costs. Moat width: wide on returns evidence, though the source is legal rather than purely commercial. Moat trend: widening. Secular force: AI infrastructure scale-out is a clear tailwind; the only terminal risk is architectural displacement by a competing power-delivery topology. Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat wide but legally sourced; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable. This is not a fear-driven value opportunity — it is a richly priced intellectual-property and growth name where the primary risk is multiple compression on any execution stumble.
skeptic_kill_fact: No single fact breaks the thesis: every load-bearing figure in the debate verifies against primary sources. The ITC Final Determination of February 13, 2025, validating patents 9,516,761 and 9,166,481 and issuing the Limited Exclusion Order is confirmed. First-quarter 2026 revenue of $113.0 million (up 20.2 percent year over year), backlog of $300.6 million (up 70 percent from the prior quarter), book-to-bill above 2.0, gross margin 55.2 percent, cash $404.2 million, no debt, and fiscal 2026 guidance of approximately $570 million all verify. The SynQor payment of $28.56 million made on March 18, 2026, verifies. The fiscal 2025 $45 million settlement verifies. The trailing twelve-month gain of approximately 494 percent off a 52-week low of $41.76 and a current market capitalization near $14.8 billion at roughly 107 times trailing earnings verify. The thesis is a cautious 'pass for now' — the price has front-run an already-fired catalyst at a valuation above even the base-case sum-of-the-parts estimate — and that bear case survives verification: the ITC re-rating is spent, the stock has quintupled, the valuation (roughly 107 times trailing earnings, roughly 26 times fiscal 2026 estimated revenue) sits above the roughly $215 base-case sum-of-the-parts, the royalty annuity is explicitly 'through 2026' with new deals gated on a distant 2027 ITC resolution, and Vicor itself paid SynQor $28.6 million — verified in the quarterly filing. However, the bear's single most-cited earnings-quality argument is factually incorrect and must be corrected (see corrections below), so the thesis is verified with corrections, not on the bear's own stated numbers.
skeptic_corrections: A load-bearing numerical error appears in the bear thesis, interrogator dossier, moderator conclusion, the risky-window analysis, and the where-we-disagree-with-the-market section: the claim that 'first-quarter 2026 earnings per share of $0.44 were flattered by a $27.3 million tax benefit (pre-tax operating income only approximately $16.9 million)' is false per the first-quarter 2026 quarterly report (Form 10-Q) and the earnings press release. Actual figures: pre-tax income was $20.403 million; the income-tax benefit was only $273 thousand — not $27.3 million, a roughly 100-times digit-transposition error. Net income was $20.664 million; diluted earnings per share were $0.44. Operating income was $16.884 million; the '$16.9 million' figure cited in the debate refers to operating income, not pre-tax income. First-quarter 2026 earnings were thus essentially fully operationally earned and were not meaningfully flattered by the tax line. Secondary correction: royalty revenue was disclosed separately in the quarterly filing ($14.965 million in first-quarter 2026), which contradicts the claim that 'management bundles the royalty versus product split.' Net effect: earnings quality is better than the bear alleged, which modestly raises the productive earnings floor. However, this does not change the valuation or already-fired-catalyst conclusions, so the cautious verdict and low conviction remain intact. The fiscal 2025 $45 million one-time settlement and the 23 percent royalty step-up that flattered fiscal 2025 revenue growth of 26 percent remain valid bear points and are independently verified.
skeptic_evidence: Vicor IR / GlobeNewswire, 2025-02-14: 'ITC bars importation of power modules and unlicensed computing systems that infringe Vicor patents' — Inv. No. 337-TA-1370, U.S. Patents 9,516,761 & 9,166,481 found valid/infringed, Limited Exclusion Order + Cease-and-Desist issued (load-bearing catalyst, already FIRED).

Vicor Q1 2026 10-Q (qtr ended 2026-03-31, via StockTitan SEC-filings summary): net revenue $113.0M, pre-tax income $20.403M, income-tax BENEFIT $273K, net income $20.664M, diluted EPS $0.44, operating income $16.884M, GM 55.2% ($62.366M), royalty revenue $14.965M, cash $404.245M, no long-term debt — REFUTES the '$27.3M tax benefit' bear claim.

Vicor Q1 2026 10-Q / earnings call (2026-05): SynQor judgment of $28,557,256 paid 2026-03-18 (Federal Circuit affirmed 2026-02-13); Q1'26 operating cash flow -$3.9M net of that payment — confirms litigation cuts both ways and the royalty annuity is two-sided.

Market data (Yahoo/CNN, June 2026): VICR $328.04, market cap ~$14.8B, trailing P/E ~107x, 52-wk range $41.76-$361.89 (~+494% TTM) — confirms valuation sits above the ~$215 base-case SoP and the already-ran/already-fired risk.

scale.role: High-density 48V/factorized power-conversion modules for AI racks; differentiated but small and contested.
scale.rationale: Real differentiated tech (factorized power, ITC exclusion order, B:B>2.0, backlog +70% QoQ) but small and losing socket share to MPWR/merchant silicon is the live franchise risk — a TACTICAL expression. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, and the headline correction cuts FOR the name (the bear's '$27.3M tax benefit' earnings-quality kill was a ~100x digit-transposition; Q1 was operationally earned), so a small ~8pt haircut off a ~48 franchise. The run already happened (base ~$215 is ~34% below spot, ~107x P/E) — that lives in valuation_posture; wait.
scale.demand_durability: Medium — AI-rack power modules, but losing socket share to MPWR/merchant silicon is the live risk.
